The methodology transfers; the exact coefficients do not. Applying the same models to Geneva or Bern would require refitting on local data.

13.1.4 Next Steps

13.2 Businesscase conclusion

13.2.1 Recommendations for Hosts

For hosts setting or revising their nightly price:

  • Anchor on structure, not status. What a guest actually pays for is the listing itself: room type, capacity, bathrooms, location. The Superhost badge helps with visibility and booking volume, not with the per-night rate.
  • Use the traffic-light tool as a sanity check, not a target. The GAM-based recommendation gives a fair-market range based on what comparable listings achieve. A listing flagged “Underpriced” is leaving money on the table; one flagged “Overpriced” risks sitting empty.
  • If you’re building toward Superhost status, compete on price. The 2.6× occupancy boost comes from satisfied guests and a full calendar. Premium pricing makes both harder. Start with competitive pricing, accumulate reviews, then raise prices selectively once the badge is secure.
  • Review scores matter less than you think for price. Across the pricing models the reward for higher ratings is modest. Reviews matter, but their role is to drive bookings.

13.2.2 Recommendations for Airbnb

For the platform itself, the same data points in three directions:

  • A pricing assistant should use the GAM, not a linear formula. Linear or postcode-based tools will systematically underprice lakeshore listings and overprice rural ones. The 2D spatial spline is the most defensible way to capture local dynamics with the data Airbnb already collects.
  • Coach the “Underpriced” segment. Roughly 41% of listings in the dataset sit below the GAM’s fair-price band. These hosts are the easiest group to help: a recommendation alone could shift their pricing toward market value, lifting both host revenue and platform commission.
  • Add operationalised location features. Raw coordinates work well for a smoother like the GAM but limit simpler tools. Distance to the nearest lake, city centre, ski lift, and public transport stop would let classifiers like the SVM catch up to the GAM’s accuracy — and these features are trivial to compute centrally and serve to every model on the platform.

14 Lessons learned

14.1 Pascal

Working on this project was a huge reality check in the best way possible. In our classes, we are used to getting “perfect” datasets that are practically made for the specific algorithms we are learning. But working with our actual d.airbnb_listings_feat dataset showed me how much effort actually goes into data cleaning and prepping before you can even start modeling. I personally focused on building the GLM-Poisson and ANN models, and trying to get them to perform well on real, messy data required a lot more critical thinking and trial-and-error than our standard assignments in class.

Since we split up the workload, I didn’t code every single model myself. Instead, my role for the LM, GLM-Binomial, GAM, and SVM models was to check and review the work of my team members. This turned out to be a super valuable learning experience on its own. Reading through their code allowed me to see exactly how other people translate what we learned in class into practice. Seeing their different approaches to problem-solving and troubleshooting bugs really helped expand my own coding mindset.

Finally, figuring out how to collaborate effectively on code was a massive win for us. We used GitHub with RStudio for the first time, and it honestly made a world of difference. We finally found a setup that actually works for coding together as a group, which saved us a lot of headaches and is definitely a workflow I’ll be taking into future projects.

14.2 Timon

I was already familiar with RStudio from my bachelor’s program, and I’d had very positive experiences with it so far and I feel very comfortable using it. Up until now, however, I’ve mainly worked with simple linear models and generalized linear models. Everything else was new to me. In this project, I focused primarily on support vector machines, and I’m very impressed by the concept because it’s both very simple and yet involves a tremendous amount of complexity. Working with this model in particular was a key learning experience for me.

Another key learning was the SCRUM-like structuring of the work process. Within the document, we work with a list of tasks to be completed by the next meeting and a backlog of tasks that we can’t tackle right now but know are coming up. This worked very well for us. It provided a sense of accountability to ensure all tasks were completed effectively.

Finally, working with GitHub on this scale was also fairly new to me. We used the integration with RStudio for the first time, and I’m personally thrilled by how simple it is. We had almost no issues with incorrect versions, even though we weren’t working with separate branches. I think, however, that splitting up the models really helped us in that regard.

14.3 Davide

Coming into this project, my comfort zone was firmly in theory. I knew what a GLM was on paper, but actually building one that holds up against messy data was a different exercise. Working on the GLM Binomial and GAM forced me to confront the gap between understanding a model and making it useful. Things that look elegant in a lecture slide turn out to require dozens of small judgement calls in practice: which variables to include, how to handle NAs, when to log-transform, where to draw a threshold.

The GAM was probably my biggest single learning. I had never worked with smooth terms before, and seeing the 2D spatial spline pick up Vaud’s geography was genuinely a moment where the theory clicked into practice.

Another lesson, less technical but probably the most useful one, was around our several iterations and the extra miles we took. I rewrote most of my texts at least three times. Each round made them shorter, clearer, and less AI-flavoured. I went into this project assuming the modelling would be the hard part. By the end, communicating the results clearly turned out to be just as much work.

Finally, R Bootcamp in February laid the foundation, but this project was where I actually got comfortable. Reading the others’ code, debugging together, learning shortcuts — that side-by-side learning was something I can’t get from a tutorial.

15 Use of GenAI

Throughout this machine learning project, we leveraged various Generative AI tools specifically ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude to support our analytical workflow. A primary use case was troubleshooting complex issues that extended beyond our existing knowledge base. When encountering technical bottlenecks, we used these models to break down and explain the underlying concepts, enabling us to systematically find the right solutions. Furthermore, we utilized GenAI as a critical review mechanism for our modeling approach. We consulted the AI to verify whether we had made the appropriate theoretical assumptions, such as selecting the correct data distributions for our models. It also served as a valuable sounding board to double-check our final analytical interpretations, ensuring our conclusions were statistically sound. Finally, these tools provided essential linguistic support. We actively used GenAI to translate our documentation and findings into professional English, ensuring a high-quality final report.
